title: Generics & Type Erasure
summary: How Java generics work under the hood, type erasure, wildcards, PECS, and bridge methods.

sections.title: Generics Compile-Time vs Runtime
sections.content:
graph LR
    A["Generic Code\nList<String>"] -->|javac| B["Type Checking\n(compile time)"]
    B -->|type erasure| C["Bytecode\nList (raw type)"]
    C -->|JVM runs| D["Runtime\nno type info"]
    style B fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style D fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: Cannot do new T() or T[]
sections.content: Because of type erasure, you cannot instantiate a type parameter directly: `new T()` or `new T[]` won't compile. Work around with Class<T> tokens: pass `Class<T> clazz` and call `clazz.newInstance()`.

sections.title: PECS — Producer Extends, Consumer Super
sections.content:
graph TD
    PECS[PECS Rule]
    PECS --> PE["Producer? Use extends\n? extends T\nYou READ from it"]
    PECS --> CS["Consumer? Use super\n? super T\nYou WRITE to it"]
    PE --> Ex1["copy(src: List<? extends T>)\nSRC produces values"]
    CS --> Ex2["copy(dst: List<? super T>)\nDST consumes values"]
    style PECS fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style PE fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style CS fill:#d97706,color:#fff

sections.question: What does PECS stand for and what does it guide?
options: Producer Extends, Consumer Super — guides wildcard selection
Private Encapsulation Compile Safety — guides access modifiers
Polymorphic Erasure Compile Strategy — guides type erasure
Producer Enum, Consumer Sealed — guides modern Java features
sections.explanation: PECS (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) is the guideline for choosing wildcards: use `? extends T` when a collection produces values you read from it, and `? super T` when it consumes values you write to it.
